title: Whittled oak wax seal handle
subtitle: Turned-form wax seal handle carved from seasoned oak
excerpt: A wax seal handle carved from a small piece of seasoned oak: a cylinder grip that fits the palm, a flat striking face, and a simple monogram or motif cut into the face with a detail knife. The metal seal stamp presses into the base; this project is about the wooden handle that makes the stamp usable.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. II (1885) for small turned and carved wooden handles; seal-handle carving is documented in Victorian craft manuals.

glossaryTerms.definition: Air-dried or kiln-dried timber at a stable moisture content; 8 to 12 per cent for indoor use. A seal handle needs seasoned wood to avoid any warping after shaping.
The rough-shaped piece of wood from which the finished item is carved.
The safe default cut: blade travelling away from the body, driven by the forearm, cutting off the end of the workpiece into open air.
Three or more cuts converging to lift a small triangular chip from the surface; the chip-carving fundamental for surface decoration.

content.text: A wax seal stamp is a two-part object: the metal die that impresses the wax, and the handle that lets you grip and press it. This project is the handle. The die is purchased or commissioned from a metalwork supplier; the wooden handle is carved to fit the palm and to present the die face squarely on the wax. The handle wants
seasoned

wood; a green-wood handle will warp as it dries and the die will no longer sit square. Oak is the natural choice: it is dense and stiff, it does not mark easily in a pocket, and it takes a fine surface detail if the grain is straight.
Grip: four-finger grip on the work; thumb-push grip on the knife. Never make a cut whose blade-path can reach a body part.
Cut direction: push cuts and thumb-pivot cuts are the default. Pull cuts only with the work braced against the chest.
Sharpening: seasoned oak is harder than green wood; keep the sloyd knife stropped frequently. A dull edge on dense wood slips.
First aid: keep a styptic pencil, gauze, and a clean dressing within reach.
Workspace: stable seat, good light, no children or pets within arm-and-blade reach.
The wood

A small piece of seasoned oak: 9 cm long, 3.5 cm square cross-section, straight-grained through the full length. The grain must run along the handle axis; a cross-grained piece will split through the handle under gripping pressure. Offcuts from joinery work or kiln-dried stock from a timber merchant are both reliable sources.
Method
Mark the handle shape on the square
blank

: a cylinder 3 cm in diameter and 8 cm long, with a flat striking face at the base (where the die attaches) and a slightly domed grip at the top. The domed grip sits in the palm more naturally than a flat end.
Round off the four corners of the square blank with
push cuts
along the long axis, four-finger grip on the blank. Work from an octagon to a sixteen-sided shape to a near-cylinder in successive passes. The aim is a smooth cylinder without flats or high spots.

Shape the dome at the top with thumb-pivot cuts: the off-hand thumb on the back of the blade, blade arcing over the dome. The dome should be no more than 5 mm above the cylinder face at its highest point.

Mark the recess for the die on the flat base. The die shaft diameter determines the recess diameter; measure the die shaft with calipers and mark a circle of that diameter centred on the base. Carve the recess with the detail knife: stop cuts around the perimeter, then waste removal from the centre outward. The recess should be 8 to 10 mm deep for a secure fit.
Optional surface decoration:
chip cuts
around the band where the dome meets the cylinder add a decorative detail. Three-cut chips, each 5 mm on a side, in a simple border ring.
Finishing

Sand through 120, 180, and 220 grit. Apply Danish oil in three thin coats, sanding lightly with 320 grit between coats two and three. Danish oil builds a semi-hard surface that resists the wax drips inevitable in use.
Variations
Walnut: a darker grip that contrasts well with a brass die.
Faceted handle: rather than rounding to a full cylinder, stop at an octagonal cross-section. The flats make the handle easier to grip and give space for chip-carved panels.
Care
Wipe wax drips off the handle with a dry cloth while still warm. Re-oil once a year.

items.symptom: Cylinder has flat spots after rounding
items.cause: Push cuts stopped short rather than continuing in an even arc around the blank
items.fix: Rotate the blank a quarter-turn and re-check. Flat spots show as lighter areas in raking light. Take one more pass with the sloyd knife across the flat, maintaining contact across the full curve.

items.cause: Circle not marked with dividers from the centre point of the base
items.fix: Mark the centre point of the base with a bradawl or pencil point before drawing the recess circle. The centre point gives the stop-cut perimeter its reference.
